labels.name: backend
labels.description: Backend related

prs.body:
## Summary
Enables frontends and dispatchers to retrieve the complete telemetry/milestone event history for a trip, enabling route-history plotting and delivery auditing.

Closes #868

## Changes

### `backend/api/src/routes/tripRoutes.js`
- Implement `GET /api/trips/:id/events`:
  - Fetches all events from `trip_events` for the given trip ID, ordered by `event_timestamp ASC`
  - **Access control**: trip driver (`trip_events.user_id`) or order customer (`orders.customer_id`) or admin. Returns **403** for anyone else
  - Returns **404** if no events and no matching order exist for the trip ID
  - Supports optional `?type=gpsUpdate` query param for event-type filtering (applied in-process after fetching)

### `backend/api/src/validation/requestSchemas.js`
- **Bug fix**: Remove duplicate `updateProfileSchema` export (parse error on main)

### `backend/api/test/integration/tripRoutes.test.js`
- 6 new integration tests: 404 unknown trip, driver access, 403 unauthorized, customer access via order, admin bypass, type filter

## Test Results
All 16 trip route tests pass (10 pre-existing + 6 new).

prs.body:
## Summary
Drivers can now register trucks they own and list them through the API, enabling multi-truck management.

Closes #867

## Changes

### `backend/api/src/validation/requestSchemas.js`
- Add `registerTruckSchema` — validates `name` (2-100 chars), `number_plate` (Indian format regex, auto-uppercased), `max_capacity_tons` (positive, ≤100)
- **Bug fix**: Remove duplicate `updateProfileSchema` export (parse error on main)

### `backend/api/src/routes/truckRoutes.js`
- **`POST /api/trucks`**: Driver-only endpoint to register a truck
  - Validates body with `registerTruckSchema`
  - Checks for duplicate number plates before insert → 409 Conflict
  - Stores `owner_id = req.user.id` to associate truck with driver
- **`GET /api/trucks`**: Driver-only endpoint to list owned trucks
  - Filters by `owner_id`, ordered by `created_at DESC`

### `backend/api/test/integration/truckRoutes.test.js` [NEW]
- 10 integration tests: role guard, happy-path register, uppercase normalisation, validation rejections (missing fields, bad plate format, zero capacity), duplicate plate 409, empty listing, owner-filtered listing

## Test Results
All 10 truck route tests pass.

## Summary
Implements `GET /api/profile/driver/statement` so authenticated drivers can query a detailed breakdown of their historical earnings.

Closes #866

## Changes

### `backend/api/src/validation/requestSchemas.js`
- Add `driverStatementSchema` — optional `start_date` / `end_date` ISO date string query params with `.strict()`

### `backend/api/src/routes/profileRoutes.js`
- Implement `GET /api/profile/driver/statement` endpoint:
  - Protected by `requireRole(['driver'])`
  - Accepts optional `start_date` / `end_date` query filters
  - Queries `orders` filtered by `driver_id` and status in `[delivered, payment_released]`
  - Computes summary: `total_trips`, `total_base_freight`, `total_platform_fees`, `total_toll_estimate`, `total_net_earnings`

### `backend/api/src/middleware/validate.js`
- **Bug fix**: Use `Object.defineProperty` to override `req.query` — in newer Node.js versions `req.query` is a getter-only property and direct assignment silently crashes with a 500

### `backend/api/test/integration/profileRoutes.test.js`
- 3 new integration tests: 403 for non-driver, empty statement, date-filtered aggregation

## Test Results
All 11 profile route tests pass. The 2 pre-existing failures in `orders.test.js` are unrelated to this feature and exist on `main`.

prs.title: fix: ensure test isolation and mock module injection in GNN explainer tests
prs.body: Pops any cached src.inference.explainer from sys.modules during _load_explainer_module setup to guarantee mock injection, and cleans up/restores sys.modules after execution to prevent test pollution.

prs.title: fix: restore sys.modules state in cold start test to prevent downstream reinitialization errors
prs.body: Saves and restores sys.modules state for torch, librosa, and src.api.main in a finally block to prevent PyTorch reinitialization/Triton binding failures in downstream tests.

prs.title: fix: optimize circular transfer cycle detection DFS complexity
prs.body: Optimizes detect_circular_transfers cycle detection using global visited sets and recursion stack checks to prevent exponential runtime on dense transaction graphs.

prs.title: fix: prevent memory leaks by pruning centrality history in lateral movement detector
prs.body: Ensures that when nodes are evicted from the in-memory active_graph in _prune_lru_nodes, their associated deque history in centrality_history is also popped to prevent unbounded memory growth.

prs.title: fix: prevent memory leaks by evicting completed or cancelled verification codes
prs.body: Converts dictionary-based challenge storage to LRUCache (maxsize=100000) and pops keys upon successful verification, expiration, max attempts exceeded, or cancellation to prevent memory leaks.

prs.title: fix: thread-safe centrality cache operations in lateral movement detector
prs.body: Guards centrality cache accesses and mutations in update_graph and _calculate_approx_centrality with locks to prevent concurrent modification exceptions.

prs.title: fix: thread-safe Neo4j subgraph cache access
prs.body: Acquires a reentrant lock self._lock inside _invalidate_subgraph_cache_for, _get_cached_subgraph, _store_cached_subgraph, and _cleanup_expired_subgraph_cache to ensure thread-safety on OrderedDict operations.

prs.title: fix: thread-safe singleton initialization for adaptive auth engines and services
prs.body: Applies double-checked locking using threading.Lock to synchronize singleton instantiation across various adaptive auth services/engines.

prs.title: fix: thread-safe singleton initialization for adaptive auth store
prs.body: Uses double-checked locking with a thread lock to ensure get_adaptive_auth_store resolves safely under concurrent requests.

prs.title: fix: thread-safe connection pool initialization in redis client
prs.body: Ensures that get_redis_client thread-safely initializes the global Redis connection pool under concurrent requests.

prs.title: Enhancement: Add route optimization and path prediction (#1025)
prs.body:
This PR implements Route Optimization and Path Prediction for Issue #1025.

### Key Changes:
- **Pydantic Model Updates**: Added `Route` schema.
- **Route Optimization Engine**: Added grid routing, A* route optimization, traffic simulation, and route anomaly/deviation checks.
- **FastAPI Endpoints**: 
  - `POST /api/v1/geospatial/routes/optimize`: Computes optimized route on a grid with traffic multipliers.
  - `GET /api/v1/geospatial/assets/{asset_id}/predict`: Predicts the asset's next location using its active Kalman Filter tracking state.
- **Integration Tests**: Added `test_route_optimization_and_prediction` inside `tests/test_geospatial.py`.

prs.title: Bug: Geospatial data accuracy degrades over time (#1024)
prs.body:
Addresses issue #1024 by introducing calibration and noise filtering to prevent coordinate drift over time:
- Implements a 2D Kalman Filter to track positions and velocities and smooth noisy GPS inputs.
- Integrates calibration offsets (calibration_bias_lat, calibration_bias_lon) to adjust systematic sensor bias.
- Dynamically adjusts measurement noise covariance (R) based on update accuracy and signal strength.

prs.title: Enhancement: Add geofencing and boundary alerts (#1023)
prs.body:
Addresses issue #1023 by adding geofencing and boundary checking:
- Supports circular and polygonal boundaries (with coordinates encrypted at rest).
- Detects entry/exit transitions for tracked assets.
- Automatically triggers webhook calls to registered alert URLs on breach transitions.
- Exposes REST API endpoints to manage boundaries, query breach history, and register webhooks.

prs.title: Bug: Real-time geospatial updates lag significantly (#1022)
prs.body:
Addresses issue #1022 by optimizing the telemetry pipeline:
- Integrates a non-blocking asyncio memory queue for location updates.
- Groups and writes updates in batches to avoid blocking.
- Implements zlib-compressed WebSockets to broadcast updates to clients.
- Filters out minor updates using delta thresholds.

prs.title: Security: Geospatial data not encrypted at rest (#1021)

## Summary
Implements encryption for geospatial data at rest to address issue #1021.

## Related Issue
Closes #1021

## Changes Made
- Encrypts latitude and longitude using AES-256-GCM
- Implements tenant-isolated key derivation via HMAC-SHA256
- Adds audit logging for reads of coordinate data

## Notes

prs.title: feat: implement encrypted real-time geospatial asset tracking suite
prs.body:
This pull request introduces a secure, real-time, high-performance geospatial asset tracking suite, fully addressing issues #1021, #1022, #1023, #1024, and #1025.

### What We Did

1. **Security & Encryption (#1021)**:
   - Added AES-256-GCM field-level encryption for latitude and longitude coordinates.
   - Implemented tenant-isolated key derivation via HMAC-SHA256 to ensure complete isolation.
   - Added GDPR-compliant access logging for all location read operations.

2. **Real-Time Pipeline & WebSockets (#1022)**:
   - Configured an asynchronous memory queue with batch flushing to minimize blocking database writes.
   - Added WebSocket broadcasting of zlib-compressed updates to maximize bandwidth utilization.
   - Implemented delta filtering to suppress redundant updates for movements under 1.5 meters.

3. **Geofencing & Breach Detection (#1023)**:
   - Added circular boundaries (Haversine distance check) and polygonal boundaries (ray-casting point-in-polygon).
   - Maintained entry/exit state transitions to trigger breach events and post webhook notifications to registered URLs.

4. **Drift Correction & State Estimation (#1024)**:
   - Implemented a 2D Kalman Filter to smooth noisy coordinate updates and estimate asset velocities.
   - Structured calibration offsets to correct systematic sensor bias.

5. **Route Optimization & Anomaly Detection (#1025)**:
   - Leveraged NetworkX to construct grid networks with weights representing traffic/congestion.
   - Implemented A* grid routing for optimal route suggestions and calculated ETAs.
   - Added path prediction along estimated velocity vectors and route deviation alerts.

### Verification Results

All unit and integration tests passed cleanly:
- Verification of encryption and tenant isolation keys.
- Real-time queue and zlib-compressed broadcast throughput.
- Transition geofence breaches and webhook alerts.
- Kalman filtering on noisy coordinates.
- A* routing, location prediction, and path deviation checks.

## ✦ Description
Implement comprehensive API rate limiting, strict JSON request payload size limits, and clean up domain routes to enhance backend security and robustness under production loads.

Fixes #1433

---

## ⟡ Type of Change
- [ ] Bug fix (non-breaking change which fixes an issue)
- [x] New feature (non-breaking change which adds functionality)
- [ ] Breaking change (fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected)
- [ ] Documentation update (non-breaking change to docs)

---

## ✦ Description of Changes

### 1. API Rate Limiting
- Applied `express-rate-limit` middleware systematically across all standard and sensitive endpoints.
- Added and calibrated custom rate limiters:
  - `previewLimiter` (10 reqs/min) for what-if simulation and preview.
  - `assessmentLimiter` (5 reqs/min) for single assessment creation.
  - `uploadLimiter` (5 reqs/min) for CSV bulk uploads.
  - `mlLimiter` (20 reqs/min) for model versioning and dataset metrics.
  - Consolidation of `authLimiter` and `strictAuthLimiter` inside the auth router.

### 2. Payload Validation & Size Limits
- Configured a strict global request body-size limit of `10kb` on all JSON endpoints to prevent Denial-of-Service (DoS) attacks via memory exhaustion.
- Registered a route-specific override of `1mb` for the bulk import route (`POST /api/assessments/bulk`) to support large dataset ingestion.

### 3. Route Refactoring & Consolidation
- Cleaned up `server/routes.ts` by extracting monolithic inline route handlers into modular domain routers:
  - `mlRouter`
  - `exportsRouter`
  - `analyticsRouter`
  - `assessmentsRouter`
- Reordered router registrations to prevent collision from parameterized route matching (e.g. ensuring `/:id` does not capture endpoints like `/analytics` or `/export.csv`).

### 4. Tests & Quality Alignment
- Fixed test suites to match database-backed OTP attempt logic where lockout occurs on the 6th failed verification attempt.
- Updated the express-rate-limit test mock to isolate counter states per limiter instance to prevent tests from failing on shared IP limits.
- Ensured 100% of Vitest integration and unit tests pass successfully.

---

## ✦ Checklist
- [x] My code follows the style guidelines of this project.
- [x] I have performed a self-review of my code.
- [x] My changes generate no new warnings or console errors.
- [x] All automated tests pass successfully (`npm run test`).
- [x] TypeScript compilation succeeds without errors (`npm run check`).

prs.title: Add null and boolean tests for object-only keywords
prs.body:
## Description

Following #827, this adds `null` and boolean tests to `minProperties` and `propertyNames`.

Both keywords apply only to objects. A JavaScript implementation that checks `typeof data === "object"` can accidentally apply these keywords to `null`, because `typeof null === "object"`.

The existing numeric non-object tests do not catch this issue, since `typeof 12 === "number"` correctly bypasses object-only validation.

## Changes

- Added `"ignores null"` test cases.
- Added `"ignores booleans"` test cases.
- Applied across affected drafts for `minProperties` and `propertyNames`.

## Verification

- Confirmed all touched JSON files parse successfully.
- Ran `bin/jsonschema_suite check`; it fails on unrelated existing checker failures around `default` being treated as an unknown keyword in older drafts.

issues.title: [Feature]: Add GET /api/support/categories endpoint to expose valid ticket categories
issues.body:
## Summary
Frontend apps must hardcode the list of valid support ticket categories, causing drift when backend validations change. Exposing a dedicated categories endpoint keeps the frontend and backend in sync.

## Problem
The valid categories (`payment`, `order`, `technical`, `general`, `account`) are only defined inside the `supportRoutes.js` `CATEGORY_MAP`, making them invisible to clients. Any category mismatch produces silent fallback to `general`.

## Proposed Solution
**`GET /api/support/categories`** — Returns the list of valid, accepted support ticket category values.
- Public endpoint (no authentication required) so onboarding screens can populate dropdowns immediately.
- Returns `categories` array and a human-readable `labels` map.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Returns a 200 with `{ categories: [...], labels: {...} }`
- [ ] No authentication required
- [ ] Categories match the values accepted by `POST /api/support/tickets`
- [ ] Integration test verifies the response shape

## Labels
`enhancement`, `backend`

issues.title: [Feature]: Add GET /api/trips/:id/events endpoint for trip event history
issues.body:
## Summary
There is currently no way to retrieve historical trip events (GPS updates, milestones) for a completed or in-progress trip. This endpoint enables frontends to plot route history and audit trip progress.

## Problem
The `/api/trips` route has batch event ingestion (`POST /events/batch`) but lacks a read-back endpoint. Without retrieval, dispatchers and customers cannot view the route taken or audit event sequences.

## Proposed Solution
**`GET /api/trips/:id/events`** — Returns all telemetry and milestone events for the specified trip.
- Events are ordered chronologically by `event_timestamp`.
- Access is restricted to the trip's driver, the order's customer, or admin users.
- Supports optional `type` filter (e.g., `?type=gpsUpdate`) to reduce payload.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Returns `404` if the trip is not found
- [ ] Returns `403` if the requesting user is not the trip driver, customer, or admin
- [ ] Supports optional `type` query param to filter by event type
- [ ] Events are ordered by `event_timestamp ASC`
- [ ] Integration tests pass

## Labels
`enhancement`, `backend`

issues.title: [Feature]: Add truck registration and listing endpoints
issues.body:
## Summary
Drivers currently have no API to register trucks they own or list them. This feature adds two endpoints to manage trucks.

## Problem
The `/api/trucks` route lacks `POST` (create) and `GET` (list for current driver) endpoints. Drivers cannot register new trucks through the API, blocking multi-truck management.

## Proposed Solution
- **`POST /api/trucks`** — Register a new truck (name, number plate, capacity). Validated with Zod.
- **`GET /api/trucks`** — List all trucks belonging to the authenticated driver.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `POST /api/trucks` validates payload with Zod schema (plate format, name ≤ 100 chars, capacity > 0)
- [ ] Duplicate plate numbers return a `409 Conflict`
- [ ] `GET /api/trucks` returns only trucks belonging to the authenticated user
- [ ] Integration tests pass for both endpoints

## Labels
`enhancement`, `backend`

issues.title: [Feature]: Add driver statement and earnings report endpoint
issues.body: Drivers need to view a breakdown of their past earnings, including completed trips, total earnings, pending payments, and deductions (like platform fees). We should implement an endpoint GET /api/profile/driver/statement that: Accepts optional start_date and end_date query parameters. Validates these parameters as ISO date strings. Queries the driver's orders in orders (where driver_id is the current user, and status is delivered or payment_released). Computes total base freight, platform fees, toll estimate, and net earnings. Returns the computed summary along with the detailed list of matching trips.

issues.title: [Feature]: Add comments and replies system to support tickets
issues.body: Currently, support tickets only contain a subject, description, and category. There is no way for customers, drivers, or admins to write replies or comments to support tickets to have a discussion thread. We should implement a comments/replies system for support tickets: POST /api/support/tickets/:id/comments to create a comment, and GET /api/support/tickets/:id/comments to retrieve all comments on a support ticket.

Add a dedicated "Contribute to DemonTech Roadmap" section on the Home Page to encourage developers, students, and open-source enthusiasts to participate in the project.

The section should clearly explain how users can contribute and provide direct links to the GitHub repository, contribution guidelines, and open issues.

## Motivation

Currently, visitors may not immediately know that DemonTech Roadmap is an open-source project accepting contributions.

Adding a contributor-focused section on the Home Page will:

- Increase community engagement.
- Attract new contributors.
- Improve project visibility.
- Make contribution opportunities easier to discover.

---

- Add a dedicated contribution section on the Home Page.
- Include a short description about contributing.
- Add a GitHub repository button.
- Add a "View Open Issues" button.
- Add a "Read Contribution Guide" button.
- Match the existing design system and branding.

---

- [ ] Design contribution section layout.
- [ ] Add section to homepage.
- [ ] Create CTA buttons.
- [ ] Add responsive styling.
- [ ] Ensure accessibility compliance.
- [ ] Test on desktop and mobile devices.

---

## Acceptance Criteria

- Contribution section is visible on the Home Page.
- Users can easily navigate to GitHub.
- CTA buttons function correctly.
- Section is fully responsive.
- Styling is consistent with the rest of the website.

---

## Technical Notes

- Follow existing component architecture.
- Use TypeScript.
- Ensure accessibility best practices.
- Reuse existing UI components where possible.

---

`frontend`
`homepage`

`community`

issues.title: # feat: add roadmap search functionality

labels.name: good first issue
labels.description: Small, beginner-friendly work for first-time contributors.

issues.body:
# feat: add roadmap search functionality

## Description

Implement a global search feature that allows users to quickly find roadmaps, resources, guides, and documentation available on the DemonTech Roadmap platform. In Home Page.

As the number of learning resources grows, navigation through menus alone becomes inefficient. A search feature will improve discoverability and user experience.

---

## Motivation

Users should be able to find relevant content without manually browsing through multiple pages. A fast and intuitive search experience will significantly improve usability and engagement.

---

## Requirements

- Add a search input in the navbar.
- Search across roadmaps, guides, resources, and documentation.
- Support partial and case-insensitive matching.
- Display matching results instantly.
- Show a message when no results are found.
- Ensure the search experience works on both desktop and mobile devices.

---

## Tasks

- [ ] Create search input component.
- [ ] Implement search logic.
- [ ] Add support for fuzzy matching.
- [ ] Create search results dropdown/modal.
- [ ] Highlight matched content.
- [ ] Add loading and empty states.
- [ ] Ensure mobile responsiveness.
- [ ] Test search performance with larger datasets.

---

## Acceptance Criteria

- Users can search for roadmaps, resources, and guides.
- Results appear in real-time as users type.
- Search works regardless of text case.
- No significant performance issues occur.
- Search UI is responsive across devices.
- Empty state is displayed when no matches are found.

---

## Technical Notes

- Use TypeScript.
- Optimize search performance.
- Consider implementing debouncing.
- Follow existing project design patterns.

---

issues.body:
## Description

Implement a responsive mobile navigation menu for the DemonTech Roadmap website to improve navigation and usability on mobile and tablet devices.

The current navigation is primarily optimized for desktop screens. This feature will introduce a mobile-friendly hamburger menu that allows users to access all navigation links while maintaining the existing design language and accessibility standards.

---

## Motivation

As the platform grows, ensuring a seamless experience across all devices is essential. A responsive navigation system will improve accessibility, user engagement, and overall usability for mobile users.

---

## Requirements

- Add a hamburger menu button for mobile screens.
- Display navigation links inside a slide-out drawer or sheet.
- Include smooth open and close animations.
- Automatically close the menu when a link is selected.
- Preserve the current branding and visual design.
- Support keyboard navigation and screen readers.
- Ensure responsiveness across common mobile and tablet breakpoints.

---

## Tasks

- [ ] Create mobile breakpoint detection.
- [ ] Add hamburger menu component.
- [ ] Implement navigation drawer/sheet.
- [ ] Add smooth animations and transitions.
- [ ] Add accessibility attributes (ARIA labels).
- [ ] Implement keyboard navigation support.
- [ ] Test responsiveness on mobile and tablet devices.
- [ ] Verify cross-browser compatibility.

---

## Acceptance Criteria

- Navigation is fully functional on screens smaller than 768px.
- Hamburger menu opens and closes correctly.
- All navigation links are accessible from the mobile menu.
- Menu closes automatically after navigation.
- Accessibility standards are followed.
- No layout shifts or UI breaking issues occur on mobile devices.

---

## Technical Notes

- Follow existing project architecture and coding standards.
- Use TypeScript where applicable.
- Keep animations lightweight and performant.
- Ensure compatibility with current styling system.

---

issues.body:
## Problem

Clinical risk outputs, fallback recommendations, biomarker alerts, prediction explanations, and recommendation text can change without a clear golden-test safety net. This makes it hard to distinguish intentional clinical behavior updates from accidental regressions.

For a clinical decision-support project, contributors need stronger safeguards around deterministic outputs and explanation quality.

## Proposed solution

Create a golden clinical scenario test suite:

- Define a small set of representative synthetic patient scenarios covering low, moderate, high, edge-case, and missing-data profiles.
- Snapshot expected risk category, important factors, fallback disclosure, recommendation themes, and explanation structure.
- Add tests for the rule-based fallback path and clinical helper services.
- Document how to update golden scenarios when clinical logic intentionally changes.
- Ensure scenarios are synthetic and contain no PHI.

## Suggested implementation pointers

- `server/services/mlService.ts`
- `server/services/recommendation-engine.ts`
- `server/services/prediction-explainer.ts`
- `server/services/biomarker-trend-analyzer.ts`
- `server/services/assessment-quality-checker.ts`
- `tests/`
- `docs/clinical-validation.md`

## Acceptance criteria

- A documented synthetic golden dataset exists for clinical behavior tests.
- Tests cover low, moderate, high, and edge-case patient profiles.
- Fallback risk scoring and recommendation output are covered.
- Prediction explanation structure is validated without overfitting to brittle wording.
- Documentation explains how maintainers should review intentional clinical-output changes.
- No real patient data is used.

## Difficulty

Advanced

## Expected outcome

Clinical behavior becomes safer to refactor because major changes to recommendations, explanations, and fallback risk scoring are visible in tests.

issues.title: [FEATURE] Implement configurable PHI data retention and erasure workflow

labels.name: type:feature
labels.description: New features, enhancements, or requests.

labels.name: level:intermediate
labels.description: Requires standard familiarity with the codebase.

labels.name: type:security
labels.description: Vulnerability fixes or security-related enhancements.

issues.body:
## Problem

Clinical assessment data, patient-linked records, exports, and authentication-related artifacts do not appear to have a clear retention and deletion lifecycle. The app contains protected health information, so advanced contributors should help define and implement privacy-preserving lifecycle controls instead of relying only on record deletion endpoints.

Without a formal lifecycle, the project risks retaining stale PHI longer than intended and makes it difficult to support account deletion, patient data erasure, or administrative cleanup policies.

## Proposed solution

Implement a configurable data retention and erasure workflow for PHI-bearing records:

- Define retention settings for assessments, patient users, exports, audit metadata, and temporary artifacts.
- Add a backend service that can soft-delete, anonymize, or permanently purge eligible records depending on record type.
- Preserve audit-safe tombstones where needed without retaining unnecessary PHI.
- Add admin-facing or scriptable cleanup entry points with dry-run support.
- Add tests that verify patient-owned assessment records, export records, and related metadata are handled consistently.

## Suggested implementation pointers

- `shared/schema.ts`
- `server/repositories/assessment.repository.ts`
- `server/repositories/patient-user.repository.ts`
- `server/repositories/audit.repository.ts`
- `server/routes/patient.routes.ts`
- `server/routes/exports.routes.ts`
- `migrations/`
- `docs/data-privacy.md`

## Acceptance criteria

- Retention policy is documented in `docs/data-privacy.md`.
- Backend has a dedicated retention/erasure service with dry-run and execution modes.
- Patient data erasure does not leave orphaned PHI in assessment/export-related records.
- Audit-safe metadata is preserved only where needed and does not include direct PHI.
- Unit or integration tests cover retention eligibility, dry-run output, and execution behavior.
- The implementation is configurable through environment variables or a typed config module.

## Difficulty

Advanced

## Expected outcome

The project gains a privacy-aware data lifecycle suitable for a clinical application and a clear path for maintainers to manage PHI retention.

issues.title: [FEATURE] Add privacy-conscious assessment draft recovery

labels.name: type:feature
labels.description: New features, enhancements, or requests.

labels.name: level:intermediate
labels.description: Requires standard familiarity with the codebase.

issues.body:
## Problem

Assessment creation is a multi-field clinical workflow, but users can lose in-progress assessment data if the session expires, the network drops, the page reloads, or the queued assessment flow times out. This creates a poor experience and may cause clinicians to re-enter sensitive data from scratch.

Because assessment forms contain PHI, a naive local-storage autosave would also create privacy risk.

## Proposed solution

Add a privacy-conscious assessment draft recovery workflow:

- Autosave in-progress assessment input as a local draft with clear expiration.
- Avoid storing drafts indefinitely and never sync drafts to the server unless the user submits.
- Add a visible restore/discard flow when a draft is detected.
- Clear drafts after successful submission, logout, or expiration.
- Use safe storage practices and document the privacy tradeoffs.
- Add tests for draft creation, restore, discard, expiration, and successful-submit cleanup.

## Suggested implementation pointers

- `client/src/pages/Dashboard.tsx`
- `client/src/hooks/use-assessments.ts`
- `client/src/lib/apiClient.ts`
- `client/src/components/auth/AuthFlow.tsx`
- `client/src/components/ui/dialog.tsx`
- `client/src/components/ui/toast.tsx`
- `docs/data-privacy.md`

## Acceptance criteria

- In-progress assessment data can be restored after reload or temporary navigation.
- Drafts expire automatically and can be discarded manually.
- Drafts are cleared after successful assessment submission and logout.
- The UI clearly lets users restore or discard a detected draft.
- Tests cover restore, discard, expiration, and cleanup behavior.
- Privacy documentation explains where drafts are stored and when they are removed.

## Difficulty

Advanced

## Expected outcome

Clinical users get a safer, more resilient assessment workflow without introducing indefinite PHI persistence in the browser.

issues.title: [FEATURE] Add shared API contract tests for route schema drift

labels.name: type:feature
labels.description: New features, enhancements, or requests.

labels.name: level:intermediate
labels.description: Requires standard familiarity with the codebase.

labels.name: type:testing
labels.description: Adding, updating, or fixing unit/integration tests.

issues.body:
## Problem

The codebase has shared route schemas and a centralized frontend API layer, but there is no full contract test layer proving that Express responses, shared route definitions, and React consumers stay aligned over time.

As the API grows, small response-shape changes can silently break frontend parsing, assessment history, exports, or authentication flows.

## Proposed solution

Build an API contract testing workflow around the shared route definitions:

- Generate or maintain a contract map from `shared/routes.ts`.
- Add server-side tests that verify key API responses match the declared Zod response schemas.
- Add client-side tests for `ApiClient` and assessment hooks that use representative success/error payloads.
- Add CI-friendly documentation describing how contributors should update contracts when changing routes.
- Include coverage for error responses, pagination metadata, and assessment creation responses.

## Suggested implementation pointers

- `shared/routes.ts`
- `shared/routes.test.ts`
- `client/src/lib/apiClient.ts`
- `client/src/hooks/use-assessments.ts`
- `server/routes/*.ts`
- `tests/routes.test.ts`
- `tests/auth.routes.test.ts`

## Acceptance criteria

- Contract tests fail when a route returns a payload that does not match its shared schema.
- Assessment list, assessment creation, auth, export, and ML-related routes have representative contract coverage.
- Error responses are covered, not only happy paths.
- Frontend tests verify `ApiClient` behavior for JSON, empty responses, and standardized error payloads.
- Documentation explains the workflow for updating route contracts.

## Difficulty

Advanced

## Expected outcome

Frontend and backend contributors can evolve APIs safely, with automated tests catching client/server schema drift before it reaches users.

issues.title: [FEATURE] Add end-to-end request observability for assessment and ML workflows

labels.name: type:feature
labels.description: New features, enhancements, or requests.

labels.name: level:intermediate
labels.description: Requires standard familiarity with the codebase.

issues.body:
## Problem

The app has separate frontend, Express API, queue, and Python ML execution paths, but a single clinical assessment request is not traceable end-to-end. When an assessment stalls, falls back, fails validation, or gets queued, maintainers have to manually stitch together browser behavior, Express logs, queue state, and Python subprocess output.

This is especially risky for clinical workflows because failures can appear as generic "assessment failed" or fallback behavior without enough context to debug production incidents quickly.

## Proposed solution

Add request-scoped observability across the full assessment lifecycle:

- Generate or reuse a correlation/request ID at the API boundary.
- Propagate that ID through assessment creation, queue processing, ML subprocess execution, fallback handling, and response payload metadata where appropriate.
- Include structured log fields for request ID, user/session scope, route, assessment job ID, ML execution duration, fallback usage, timeout, and error class.
- Surface a support-safe request ID in frontend error states/toasts so users can report reproducible failures without exposing PHI.
- Add tests proving the ID is preserved across direct and queued assessment flows.

## Suggested implementation pointers

- `server/middleware/requestId.ts`
- `server/routes/assessments.routes.ts`
- `server/queue.ts`
- `server/services/mlService.ts`
- `server/logger.ts`
- `client/src/hooks/use-assessments.ts`
- Existing tests under `tests/` and `server/*.test.ts`

## Acceptance criteria

- Every assessment request has a stable correlation ID from entry to response.
- Direct inference and queued inference logs include the same request/job context.
- ML fallback and timeout logs include duration, fallback status, and non-PHI error metadata.
- Frontend assessment failures display or expose a support-safe reference ID.
- Tests cover correlation propagation for successful, failed, and queued assessment paths.
- No PHI is added to logs.

## Difficulty

Advanced

## Expected outcome

Maintainers can debug production assessment incidents across frontend, API, queue, and ML layers without relying on unstructured logs or PHI.

issues.title: [FEATURE] Add Secure CSV Import Preview and Validation Workflow

labels.name: type:feature
labels.description: New features, enhancements, or requests.

labels.name: level:intermediate
labels.description: Requires standard familiarity with the codebase.

labels.name: type:UX
labels.description: Improve User Experience

issues.body:
## Description

The application supports patient assessment and lab data workflows, but CSV import should be safer and more transparent before data is accepted into the system.

Currently, users may upload CSV/lab data without a dedicated preview step that clearly shows valid rows, invalid rows, missing fields, unsafe values, and validation errors before submission.

## Proposed Feature

Add a secure CSV import preview workflow.

Before saving imported CSV data, users should see a preview table that separates:

- Valid rows
- Invalid rows
- Missing required fields
- Malformed numeric values
- Unsafe formula-like CSV values
- Duplicate patient entries, if detectable

## Expected Behavior

When a user uploads a CSV file:

1. The file is parsed safely.
2. The app validates required fields.
3. A preview screen shows row-level validation results.
4. Invalid rows are highlighted with clear error messages.
5. Users can confirm import only if there are valid rows.
6. Unsafe CSV/formula-like values are sanitized or rejected.
7. No data is saved until the user confirms the preview.

## Suggested Implementation

- Add a CSV preview endpoint or client-side preview parser.
- Reuse existing CSV validation/sanitization utilities where possible.
- Add a frontend preview table for uploaded rows.
- Add row-level validation messages.
- Disable final import when no valid rows exist.
- Add tests for valid rows, invalid rows, missing patient names, malformed numeric values, and CSV injection-like values.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] CSV upload shows a preview before import.
- [ ] Valid and invalid rows are clearly separated.
- [ ] Row-level errors are displayed.
- [ ] Unsafe formula-like values are sanitized or rejected.
- [ ] The user must confirm before data is saved.
- [ ] Import is blocked when no valid rows exist.
- [ ] Existing upload behavior remains secure.
- [ ] Tests are added for validation and preview logic.

## Type of Issue

- Feature
- Security
- Frontend
- Backend
- Testing
- Advanced

## Labels

`gssoc 26`, `feature`, `security`, `level:advanced`

labels.name: type:UX

issues.body:
## Description

Assessment History currently allows users to view individual assessment records, but there is no way to export a complete patient-level longitudinal summary.

Clinicians often need a single downloadable report that summarizes a patient's risk progression over time, latest clinical metrics, trend changes, and key risk factors. This would make the application more useful for clinical review, follow-ups, and patient communication.

## Proposed Feature

Add a patient-level PDF export from Assessment History.

The PDF should summarize all available assessments for a selected patient and include:

- Patient name and demographic information
- Latest assessment date
- Latest risk score and risk category
- Risk score trend over time
- BMI, HbA1c, and blood glucose trend summary
- Improvement or deterioration indicators
- Key risk factors from recent assessments
- Clinical summary section
- Export timestamp

## Expected Behavior

When a user selects a patient in Assessment History, they should be able to click an **Export Patient Summary PDF** button.

The generated PDF should include a clean longitudinal report using that patient’s assessment history only.

## Suggested Implementation

- Add an export button in the patient trajectory / longitudinal view.
- Reuse the existing patient-scoped assessment query.
- Create a dedicated PDF utility for patient summary reports.
- Ensure the report only includes records scoped to the authenticated user.
- Format dates, risk scores, and clinical metrics clearly.
- Add tests for summary-data preparation logic where applicable.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] A user can export a patient-level PDF summary from Assessment History.
- [ ] The PDF includes only the selected patient’s assessment records.
- [ ] The report includes latest risk category, score, and assessment date.
- [ ] The report includes trend summaries for risk score, BMI, HbA1c, and blood glucose.
- [ ] The report includes key risk factors from recent assessments.
- [ ] The export works for patients with one assessment and multiple assessments.
- [ ] Empty or missing fields are handled gracefully.
- [ ] Utility/helper tests are added where applicable.

## Type of Issue

- Feature
- Frontend
- PDF Export
- Clinical Reporting
- Advanced

## Labels

`gssoc 26`, `feature`, `frontend`, `level:advanced`

The current assessment CSV export downloads a fixed set of assessment records, but it does not support exporting only the records the user is currently viewing or filtering in Assessment History.

As the number of assessments grows, exporting all records at once can become inefficient and less useful for users who only need a specific subset of data.

Add support for paginated and filtered CSV export for assessment history.

The export API should allow users to export assessments based on filters such as:

- Patient name

- Gender
- Smoking history
- Date range
- Pagination or export limit

## Expected Behavior

When users apply filters in Assessment History, the CSV export should respect those filters and export only the matching assessment records.

For example:

GET /api/assessments/export.csv?patientName=Jane&riskCategory=HIGH&limit=100

issues.title: [BUG] CSV Export Generates a Single-Line File Due to Literal \n Row Delimiter

labels.name: type:bug
labels.description: Something isn't working or throwing errors.

issues.body:
## Description
The assessment CSV export currently joins CSV rows using the literal string `"\\n"` instead of an actual newline character. As a result, exported CSV content can appear as a single-line file with visible `\n` text between rows instead of proper row breaks.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Export assessments as CSV.
2. Open the downloaded CSV in a text editor or spreadsheet tool.
3. Observe that rows are separated by literal `\n` text rather than real line breaks.

## Expected Behavior
Each CSV record should appear on its own line using an actual newline delimiter.

## Actual Behavior
The exported CSV is generated as one long line with literal `\n` between rows.

## Affected File
`server/utils/csvExport.ts`

## Suggested Fix
Replace:

```ts
.join("\\n")

## Goal

Ensure images across the website are accessible to screen reader users by providing meaningful alternative text where needed and hiding purely decorative images from assistive technologies.

  - Logo

- [ ] Informative or meaningful images include descriptive and useful alt text.
- [ ] Avoid redundant descriptions such as "image of" or "picture of".
- [ ] Ensure screen readers receive only relevant information from visual content.
- [ ] Verify that no important information is conveyed solely through images.

## Expected Outcome

Screen reader users should receive meaningful descriptions for important visual content while decorative elements remain unobtrusive, resulting in a more accessible browsing experience.

## Additional Notes

- Follow WCAG accessibility best practices for image descriptions.
- Keep alt text concise and context-aware.
- Consider the purpose of each image rather than describing every visual detail.

# Add JSON-LD Structured Data for DemonDie

## Goal

Improve search engine understanding of DemonDie by adding JSON-LD structured data that describes the project as an organization and open-source community.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Add valid JSON-LD structured data to `index.html`.
- [ ] Include the DemonDie organization/community name.
- [ ] Include the production website URL.
- [ ] Include the GitHub organization or repository link.
- [ ] Include the Discord community link.
- [ ] Include the DemonDie logo URL.
- [ ] Ensure the structured data follows Schema.org standards.
- [ ] Validate that the JSON-LD contains no syntax or schema errors.

## Expected Outcome

Search engines should be able to better understand and categorize DemonDie as an organization and community, improving discoverability and search result quality.

## Additional Notes

- No visual UI changes are required.
- Keep the implementation lightweight and easy to maintain.
- Test the structured data using a structured data validation tool before submitting a pull request.
